scoring_rubric.3: Fully correct — accurate, complete, no hallucinations
scoring_rubric.2: Partially correct — mostly right but missing key detail or minor inaccuracy
scoring_rubric.1: Mostly wrong — fundamental misunderstanding or significant hallucination
scoring_rubric.0: Completely wrong or refused to answer

questions.question: What is the KV cache in the context of transformer inference?
questions.reference: The KV cache stores the key and value tensors computed during the attention operation for all previously processed tokens. Instead of recomputing these tensors on every new decode step, vLLM reads them from GPU HBM memory. This makes decode fast but memory-intensive — the KV cache is typically the largest consumer of VRAM after model weights.

questions.question: Why does increasing max_model_len increase VRAM usage?
questions.reference: max_model_len sets the maximum sequence length (prompt + output). A longer max sequence length means vLLM must allocate KV cache blocks for more tokens per sequence. Since KV cache size scales with (num_layers × num_heads × head_dim × max_seq_len × num_sequences), a larger max_model_len directly increases peak VRAM consumption.

questions.question: What is PagedAttention and how does it relate to the KV cache?
questions.reference: PagedAttention is vLLM's memory management technique that stores KV cache in non-contiguous fixed-size blocks (pages) rather than pre-allocating a contiguous chunk per sequence. This eliminates memory fragmentation, allows KV cache to be shared across sequences with common prefixes, and enables more efficient GPU memory utilisation — similar to how an OS manages virtual memory with pages.

questions.question: What happens when the KV cache fills up during serving?
questions.reference: When the KV cache is full, vLLM must evict KV cache blocks from existing sequences to make room for new ones. Evicted sequences must be preempted (paused) and their tokens recomputed when they resume. This causes latency spikes and reduced throughput. In practice, high KV cache utilisation (above 90%) is a warning sign that max_num_seqs or max_model_len should be reduced.

questions.question: How does the KV cache size scale with model size?
questions.reference: KV cache size scales with the number of layers, number of KV heads, and head dimension — all of which grow with model size. A 7B model has more layers and larger hidden dimensions than a 4B model, so its KV cache entries per token per sequence are larger. This means a 7B model consumes more VRAM per concurrent sequence than a 4B model at identical serving settings.

questions.question: What is KV cache quantisation and why would you use it?
questions.reference: KV cache quantisation reduces the precision of stored KV tensors from FP16/BF16 to INT8 or INT4. This reduces VRAM consumption for the cache, allowing more concurrent sequences at the same memory budget. The tradeoff is a small accuracy degradation since the cached attention values are less precise. It is commonly used when serving large models on GPUs where VRAM is the primary bottleneck.

questions.question: Why is decode memory-bandwidth bound while prefill is compute-bound?
questions.reference: During prefill, all input tokens are processed in parallel in a single large matrix multiplication — this is compute-intensive and utilises GPU tensor cores efficiently. During decode, only one new token is generated per step, requiring a small matrix multiply but reading the full KV cache for every active sequence from HBM on every step. The bottleneck shifts from compute to memory bandwidth because the KV cache reads dominate.

questions.question: How does a multiturn conversation affect KV cache size compared to a single-turn request?
questions.reference: A multiturn conversation carries conversation history (previous user and assistant turns) as part of the input. This means more input tokens to process during prefill and a larger KV cache to maintain during decode. A 3-turn conversation might have 3-5x more input tokens than a single-turn request, directly increasing KV cache VRAM usage and prefill computation time.

questions.question: What is the relationship between max_num_seqs and KV cache pressure?
questions.reference: max_num_seqs controls how many sequences vLLM decodes simultaneously. More concurrent sequences means more KV cache entries must be held in VRAM simultaneously, increasing memory pressure. If max_num_seqs is too high, VRAM fills up and vLLM starts evicting sequences. If too low, GPU decode throughput is underutilised. The optimal value depends on available VRAM after model weights.

questions.question: What is GQA (Grouped Query Attention) and how does it affect KV cache size?
questions.reference: GQA reduces the number of key and value heads while keeping the number of query heads the same. Instead of each query head having its own KV head, groups of query heads share a single KV head. This reduces KV cache size proportionally to the grouping factor — for example, GQA with 8 groups uses 8x less KV cache memory than multi-head attention with the same number of query heads. Mistral-7B uses GQA, which reduces its KV cache relative to its parameter count.

questions.question: What does gpu_memory_utilization do in vLLM?
questions.reference: gpu_memory_utilization (default 0.9) tells vLLM what fraction of total GPU VRAM to use. vLLM loads model weights first, then allocates the remaining fraction to KV cache blocks. Setting it to 0.9 means 90% of VRAM is available for weights + KV cache, leaving 10% as safety margin. Raising it allows more KV cache but risks OOM if other processes use GPU memory. Lowering it reduces concurrent capacity.

questions.question: How does prefix caching use the KV cache to reduce computation?
questions.reference: Prefix caching stores computed KV cache blocks for repeated prompt prefixes. When a new request shares a prefix with a previously seen request (e.g., the same system prompt), vLLM reuses the cached KV blocks instead of recomputing them. This eliminates the prefill computation for the shared portion, reducing TTFT for requests with common prefixes. The hit rate depends on how many requests share prefixes.

questions.question: What is MQA (Multi-Query Attention) and how does it differ from MHA and GQA?
questions.reference: MHA (Multi-Head Attention) has one KV head per query head — largest KV cache. MQA (Multi-Query Attention) has a single shared KV head for all query heads — smallest KV cache, fastest decode, but potentially lower quality. GQA (Grouped Query Attention) sits between them — groups of query heads share a KV head, balancing KV cache size and quality. Most modern models use GQA as the practical sweet spot.

questions.question: Why does a higher max_num_seqs sometimes worsen ITL?
questions.reference: Higher max_num_seqs means more sequences are decoded simultaneously. Each decode step must read the KV cache for all active sequences from HBM. More sequences means more memory bandwidth consumed per step. Once the total KV cache read demand exceeds the GPU's HBM bandwidth, each decode step takes longer to complete, directly increasing ITL. This is memory bandwidth saturation.

questions.question: What is the difference between KV cache eviction and recomputation in vLLM?
questions.reference: When vLLM runs out of KV cache space, it can either evict (discard) KV blocks from lower-priority sequences or swap them to CPU memory. Evicted sequences are preempted and must recompute their KV cache when they resume, adding latency. Swapping is slower than eviction but avoids full recomputation. Both strategies introduce latency spikes and should be avoided by correctly sizing max_num_seqs and max_model_len.

questions.question: What is TTFT and why does it matter for user experience?
questions.reference: TTFT (Time to First Token) is the elapsed time from when a request is sent to when the first token of the response is received. It determines how quickly users see the system respond. High TTFT makes the system feel unresponsive even if subsequent tokens stream quickly. For interactive applications, TTFT p95 below 1-2 seconds is typically required for acceptable user experience.

questions.question: What is ITL and what causes it to increase?
questions.reference: ITL (Inter-Token Latency) is the average time gap between consecutive tokens in a streaming response. It is determined by how long each decode step takes. ITL increases when: (1) more sequences are decoded simultaneously, increasing memory bandwidth demand; (2) the model is larger, requiring more computation per step; (3) GPU memory bandwidth is saturated from concurrent KV cache reads.

questions.question: A student sees TTFT p95 jump from 700ms to 4s when users increase from 50 to 200. What are the likely causes?
questions.reference: The most likely cause is queue congestion — at 200 users, requests are waiting longer in the vLLM scheduler queue before their prefill begins. The GPU cannot process prefills fast enough to keep up with the arrival rate. Secondary causes include: KV cache pressure causing preemptions; max_num_seqs too low causing a bottleneck; or chunked prefill interleaving adding overhead. First experiment: reduce max_tokens or max_model_len, then try increasing max_num_batched_tokens.

questions.question: What is the difference between TTFT p50 and TTFT p95, and why monitor both?
questions.reference: p50 (median) is the TTFT that 50% of requests are faster than — represents the typical user experience. p95 is the TTFT that 95% of requests are faster than — represents the worst experience for the majority. p50 can look healthy while p95 reveals tail latency problems. In production, SLOs are set on p95 or p99 because p50 hides the experiences of the worst-served users.

questions.question: How does prefill computation time affect TTFT?
questions.reference: TTFT = queue wait time + prefill computation time. For short prompts, prefill is fast and queue wait dominates TTFT. For long prompts (e.g., multiturn history, long documents), prefill computation itself becomes significant — the GPU must process hundreds or thousands of input tokens before generating the first output token. Chunked prefill can reduce the impact of long prefills on TTFT by interleaving prefill with decode.

questions.question: Why might ITL spike suddenly at a certain concurrency level even though error rate stays at 0%?
questions.reference: A sudden ITL spike with zero errors indicates memory bandwidth saturation, not queue overflow. All requests are being served but the GPU's HBM bandwidth is exhausted by simultaneous KV cache reads across too many concurrent decode sequences. The fix is to reduce max_num_seqs so fewer sequences decode simultaneously, reducing bandwidth demand per step. This is distinct from a queue overflow (which causes errors or high TTFT) — ITL spikes are a decode-phase phenomenon.

questions.question: What vLLM metric would you monitor to detect KV cache pressure before it causes errors?
questions.reference: Monitor vllm:gpu_cache_usage_perc — the percentage of KV cache blocks currently in use. When this exceeds 85-90%, vLLM is close to evicting sequences. Alert at 80% to give time to react. Also monitor vllm:num_requests_waiting — a growing waiting queue indicates the scheduler cannot keep up. Both metrics are exposed in Prometheus format at the /metrics endpoint.

questions.question: How do you distinguish a TTFT problem caused by queue wait vs prefill computation?
questions.reference: Compare TTFT across different prompt lengths at the same concurrency. If TTFT scales with prompt length (longer prompts → proportionally higher TTFT), prefill computation dominates. If TTFT is similar regardless of prompt length, queue wait dominates. Also check vllm:num_requests_waiting — a high value indicates queue congestion. Chunked prefill helps when prefill dominates; adding replicas helps when queue congestion dominates.

questions.question: Why is TTFT typically worse at p99 than p95, and what causes the tail?
questions.reference: p99 captures the slowest 1% of requests. These tail latency outliers are usually caused by: (1) KV cache evictions forcing expensive recomputation; (2) occasional GPU scheduling jitter; (3) requests that arrive just as the queue peaks; (4) unusually long prompts that monopolise prefill slots. The gap between p95 and p99 indicates how severe and frequent these outlier events are.

questions.question: A team's TTFT p95 is good at 800ms but their users complain the response feels slow. What might be wrong?
questions.reference: Good TTFT p95 with poor perceived quality often means ITL is too high. If ITL is 50ms per token, a 100-token response takes 5 seconds to stream even with an 800ms first token. Users experience the streaming as sluggish or stuttering. The solution is to investigate and reduce ITL — check max_num_seqs for bandwidth saturation, consider reducing concurrent decode load, or use a smaller/quantised model.

questions.question: How does torch.compile (no-fast-boot mode) affect ITL compared to eager mode?
questions.reference: torch.compile optimises GPU kernels for the decode loop, producing compiled CUDA code that runs faster than the interpreted eager execution. In practice this reduces ITL by 60-75% — from ~20-25ms in eager mode to 5-7ms in compiled mode for typical models. The tradeoff is a long warmup time (5-15 minutes) on first boot as kernels are compiled. The compiled kernels are cached in the vLLM volume for subsequent runs.

questions.question: What does aggregate_stream_chunks_per_s measure and how does it relate to ITL?
questions.reference: aggregate_stream_chunks_per_s is the total number of streaming chunks (tokens) received per second across all concurrent users. It is a throughput measure at the system level. ITL measures the per-token latency for a single request. They are related: if ITL is 10ms per token and 50 users are each receiving tokens, aggregate throughput would be approximately 50 / 0.010 = 5,000 chunks/s, though in practice batching and queuing make the relationship less direct.

questions.question: Why does TTFT grow faster than linearly with user count past the saturation point?
questions.reference: Past saturation, each new user adds to the queue depth. But as the queue grows, new arrivals wait behind all existing queued requests. Queue wait time grows roughly proportionally to queue depth, which itself grows with users. Additionally, as more sequences decode simultaneously, each decode step takes longer (bandwidth pressure), slowing the rate at which the queue drains. Both effects compound, causing TTFT to grow super-linearly with users past the sweet spot.

questions.question: What is the practical difference between monitoring TTFT and end-to-end latency?
questions.reference: TTFT measures only the delay to the first token — the responsiveness signal. End-to-end latency measures the total time from request to final token — the completion signal. For streaming applications, users perceive TTFT first (does it respond?) and then ITL (does it stream smoothly?). End-to-end latency matters more for batch or non-streaming use cases. In production, monitor all three: TTFT, ITL, and end-to-end latency, as they reveal different failure modes.

questions.question: How would you reduce TTFT for a workload with very long system prompts?
questions.reference: The most effective approach is prefix caching — since the system prompt is identical across requests, its KV cache is computed once and reused. This eliminates the system prompt from prefill computation entirely. Additionally: enable chunked prefill to prevent long prefills from blocking decode; reduce max_model_len if the system prompt does not require the full context window; consider system prompt compression techniques to reduce token count.

questions.question: What is continuous batching and how does it differ from static batching?
questions.reference: Static batching waits for a full batch of requests before starting inference, and all requests in the batch must finish before the next batch starts. Continuous batching (used by vLLM) processes requests as they arrive — when one sequence finishes, a new one is immediately inserted into the freed slot without waiting. This keeps GPU utilisation high and reduces latency for shorter requests that would otherwise wait for longer ones to finish.

questions.question: What does max_num_batched_tokens control in vLLM?
questions.reference: max_num_batched_tokens sets the maximum total number of tokens (across all sequences) that vLLM processes in a single forward pass. It bounds the size of the prefill batch. A higher value means more tokens processed per step, improving prefill throughput. However, very large values increase GPU memory usage per step and can cause OOM. It should be set based on available VRAM and the expected prompt length distribution.

questions.question: Why does increasing max_num_batched_tokens improve throughput but can worsen ITL?
questions.reference: Larger batches process more tokens per forward pass, improving prefill throughput. However, if a large prefill batch occupies the GPU for a long time, it delays decode steps for already-running sequences — increasing ITL. Chunked prefill addresses this by breaking large prefills into smaller chunks interleaved with decode, balancing throughput and ITL. Without chunked prefill, max_num_batched_tokens is a direct tradeoff between prefill speed and decode latency.

questions.question: What is the role of the vLLM scheduler?
questions.reference: The vLLM scheduler decides which sequences to process in each forward pass — which prefills to run, which decodes to continue, and which requests to queue or preempt. It balances KV cache utilisation, request priority, and GPU throughput. The scheduler implements continuous batching, manages KV cache block allocation, and handles preemption when memory pressure is high. Its decisions directly determine TTFT, ITL, and overall throughput.

questions.question: How does the scheduler decide to preempt a sequence?
questions.reference: vLLM preempts sequences when KV cache blocks run out and a new higher-priority request needs to be served. The scheduler selects sequences to preempt based on policies (typically last-in-first-out or least-recently-used). Preempted sequences have their KV cache blocks freed or swapped to CPU memory, and must recompute or reload their KV cache when resumed. Preemptions cause latency spikes for the affected sequences.

questions.question: What is the difference between max_num_seqs and max_num_batched_tokens?
questions.reference: max_num_seqs limits how many sequences are in the running state simultaneously (being decoded). max_num_batched_tokens limits the total tokens processed across all sequences in one forward pass. They control different bottlenecks: max_num_seqs limits concurrent decode and KV cache pressure; max_num_batched_tokens limits prefill throughput and per-step GPU compute. Both must be tuned together — a high max_num_seqs with low max_num_batched_tokens creates a scheduling bottleneck.

questions.question: Why does throughput not always scale linearly with max_num_seqs?
questions.reference: Throughput scales with max_num_seqs up to the point where memory bandwidth or KV cache capacity becomes the bottleneck. Beyond that point, adding more concurrent sequences increases memory bandwidth pressure per step (more KV reads), slowing each decode step. The throughput gain from more parallelism is offset by the slowdown in each step. Additionally, more sequences mean more KV cache VRAM consumption, potentially causing evictions that further hurt throughput.

questions.question: What is dynamic batching and how does it differ from vLLM's continuous batching?
questions.reference: Dynamic batching groups requests that arrive within a time window into a batch before sending them for inference. It is still fundamentally a static batch model — requests wait for the window to fill. vLLM's continuous batching runs a continuous loop where sequences are added and removed from the running set as they arrive and complete, without any fixed time window. Continuous batching has lower latency for individual requests and higher GPU utilisation.

questions.question: How does the arrival rate of requests affect scheduling behaviour?
questions.reference: At low arrival rates, each request is processed almost immediately — minimal queue wait. As arrival rate approaches the server's throughput capacity, the queue grows and wait times increase. When arrival rate exceeds throughput capacity, the queue grows unboundedly and TTFT increases without limit. The system reaches a stable operating point only when arrival rate is below maximum throughput. This is why load testing at various user counts is essential — to find the stable operating region.

questions.question: What happens to short requests when long requests dominate the batch?
questions.reference: Without careful scheduling, long requests (long prompts or long outputs) can monopolise GPU time, causing head-of-line blocking for short requests waiting in the queue. Short requests accumulate high TTFT even though they would complete quickly if served. Chunked prefill mitigates this for prefill-heavy requests by breaking them into smaller chunks. Priority scheduling can further help by giving short requests preferential treatment.

questions.question: What is the concurrent_inputs parameter in Modal and how does it relate to vLLM's max_num_seqs?
questions.reference: concurrent_inputs is a Modal-level gateway setting that controls how many requests Modal will hold per container (queued + being processed). It is the outer queue before requests reach vLLM. max_num_seqs is vLLM's internal limit on simultaneous decode sequences. For the system to work without dropping requests, concurrent_inputs must be >= max_num_seqs plus a buffer for requests waiting to enter vLLM. Setting concurrent_inputs < max_num_seqs causes Modal to reject requests that vLLM could have served.

questions.question: How does request timeout interact with scheduling under high load?
questions.reference: Under high load, requests spend time in the queue before processing begins. If the queue wait plus processing time exceeds the client's request timeout, the client cancels the request. From the server's perspective, the request may still be processing, wasting GPU compute on a request whose response will be discarded. Long timeouts reduce client errors under load but can waste server resources. Short timeouts protect clients but increase error rates under load.

questions.question: What is the effect of the ramp-up period in load testing?
questions.reference: Ramp-up gradually increases concurrent users from 0 to the target over a set time period. Without ramp-up, all users arrive simultaneously, creating a thundering herd — a sudden spike of requests that overwhelms the queue, causes high TTFT, and potentially triggers preemptions. Ramp-up spreads arrivals over time, allowing the server to reach steady state gradually and produce stable, representative metrics rather than spike-dominated measurements.

questions.question: Why does goodput differ from raw throughput?
questions.reference: Raw throughput is total tokens or chunks produced per second regardless of whether the responses were actually delivered successfully. Goodput is throughput weighted by success rate: goodput = throughput * (1 - error_rate). A system that produces 5,000 chunks/s but has a 50% error rate has a goodput of only 2,500 chunks/s — half the work was wasted. Goodput is the production-relevant metric because it measures useful work delivered to users, not total GPU activity.

questions.question: How would you determine the maximum sustainable request rate for a given vLLM configuration?
questions.reference: Run a series of load tests at increasing request rates (or user counts with a fixed pause pattern). Plot TTFT p95 and error rate against request rate. The maximum sustainable rate is the highest rate where error rate remains near 0% and TTFT p95 stays within the SLO. Beyond this point TTFT climbs sharply or errors appear. Add 20% headroom below this ceiling for the production operating point to handle burst traffic.

questions.question: What is quantisation in the context of LLM inference?
questions.reference: Quantisation reduces the numerical precision of model weights from the training precision (usually FP32 or BF16) to a lower precision (INT8, INT4, FP8). This reduces model size in VRAM, increases throughput (smaller matrices multiply faster), and reduces memory bandwidth consumption. The tradeoff is a small accuracy degradation. Common methods include GPTQ, AWQ, and SmoothQuant, each with different accuracy-speed tradeoffs.

questions.question: What is the difference between BF16 and FP16 and why does vLLM prefer BF16?
questions.reference: Both BF16 and FP16 use 16 bits but with different bit allocations. FP16 has higher precision (10 mantissa bits, 5 exponent bits). BF16 has lower precision (7 mantissa bits) but a wider dynamic range (8 exponent bits, same as FP32). BF16 is preferred for LLM inference because the wide dynamic range prevents numerical overflow/underflow during computation, matching FP32's range. H100 GPUs have native BF16 tensor cores, making BF16 both numerically stable and fast.

questions.question: How much VRAM does a 7B parameter model require in BF16?
questions.reference: Each BF16 parameter uses 2 bytes. A 7B parameter model requires approximately 7 × 10^9 × 2 bytes = 14GB of VRAM just for model weights. This excludes KV cache, activations, and framework overhead. On an H100 80GB GPU, a 7B BF16 model leaves about 66GB for KV cache and other overhead — more than sufficient for moderate concurrency.

questions.question: What is INT8 quantisation and what accuracy tradeoff does it introduce?
questions.reference: INT8 quantisation stores model weights as 8-bit integers instead of 16-bit floats, halving VRAM usage. During computation, weights are dequantised back to FP16/BF16. The accuracy tradeoff is typically small (1-3% degradation on benchmarks) for most models and tasks. INT8 is generally safe for production use. Methods like SmoothQuant and LLM.int8() maintain most accuracy while halving memory requirements.

questions.question: What is the difference between weight quantisation and activation quantisation?
questions.reference: Weight quantisation reduces precision of stored model parameters (weights). Activation quantisation reduces precision of intermediate tensor values computed during inference. Weight-only quantisation is simpler and most commonly used — activations remain in high precision. Full quantisation (weights + activations) achieves greater speed improvements but is harder to implement without accuracy loss because activation distributions vary widely across inputs.

questions.question: What is AWQ quantisation and how does it differ from GPTQ?
questions.reference: AWQ (Activation-aware Weight Quantisation) identifies which weights are most important based on activation magnitudes and protects them from quantisation loss. It achieves better accuracy than naive INT4 at the same bit width. GPTQ uses a second-order optimisation method to minimise quantisation error weight by weight. Both target INT4 weights. AWQ is generally faster to apply and often achieves comparable or better accuracy than GPTQ at INT4.

questions.question: How does GPU memory utilisation affect KV cache size in vLLM?
questions.reference: vLLM uses gpu_memory_utilization (default 0.9) to determine how much VRAM to use. It loads model weights first, then allocates the remaining VRAM (up to the utilisation fraction) to KV cache blocks. Higher utilisation = more KV cache blocks = more concurrent sequences supported. But leaving too little headroom risks OOM from activation memory spikes during large prefills. 0.9 is the recommended default; lower it to 0.85 if OOM errors occur.

questions.question: What is HBM and why does it matter for LLM serving?
questions.reference: HBM (High Bandwidth Memory) is the on-chip memory technology used in GPUs like H100. It provides extremely high memory bandwidth (H100 has ~3.35 TB/s HBM3 bandwidth) compared to traditional DRAM. LLM decode is memory-bandwidth bound — each decode step reads the full KV cache from HBM. Higher HBM bandwidth directly enables faster decode (lower ITL) and supports more concurrent sequences before bandwidth saturation. HBM bandwidth is one of the most important specs for LLM serving hardware.

questions.question: Why does quantising a model not always proportionally improve throughput?
questions.reference: Quantisation reduces model size and can speed up matrix multiplications, but throughput depends on multiple bottlenecks. If the system is memory-bandwidth bound (KV cache reads), quantising weights does not help much because the KV cache reads still dominate. If the system is compute-bound (large prefill batches), quantisation helps more. In practice, gains depend on which bottleneck is active at the operating concurrency level.

questions.question: What is FP8 and how does it compare to INT8 for LLM inference?
questions.reference: FP8 is an 8-bit floating point format (E4M3 or E5M2 variants) that maintains a floating point representation unlike INT8. FP8 inference is supported natively on H100 GPUs and provides similar memory savings to INT8 with potentially better accuracy for sensitive operations. H100's FP8 tensor cores can achieve roughly 2x the throughput of BF16 tensor cores for compute-bound workloads. vLLM supports FP8 via the --dtype fp8 flag.

questions.question: How does tensor parallelism affect GPU memory usage per device?
questions.reference: Tensor parallelism shards model weights across multiple GPUs. Each GPU holds only 1/N of the total weights (where N is the tensor parallel degree). This reduces per-GPU weight memory by N. However, KV cache is also sharded across GPUs, so each GPU holds 1/N of the KV cache blocks. The tradeoff is that all-reduce communication between GPUs adds latency to every forward pass.

questions.question: What does OOM (Out of Memory) mean in vLLM serving and what causes it?
questions.reference: OOM occurs when vLLM tries to allocate more VRAM than is available. Common causes: max_model_len too large (large KV cache allocation); max_num_seqs too high (too many concurrent KV caches); gpu_memory_utilization too high (no buffer for activation spikes); model too large for the GPU. To resolve: reduce max_model_len, reduce max_num_seqs, lower gpu_memory_utilization, or use a smaller/quantised model.

questions.question: What is speculative decoding and how does it affect VRAM usage?
questions.reference: Speculative decoding uses a small draft model to speculatively generate multiple tokens, then verifies them in parallel with the main model. If accepted, multiple tokens are generated per main model forward pass, improving throughput. VRAM impact: the draft model must be loaded in addition to the main model, increasing total VRAM usage. The draft model is typically 10-20x smaller than the main model, so the overhead is manageable. In vLLM, speculative decoding is enabled via --speculative-model.

questions.question: Why might a team choose an A100 over an H100 for LLM serving?
questions.reference: A100 costs significantly less than H100 (roughly 40-60% cheaper per hour). For models that fit comfortably in A100 VRAM (80GB variant) and where workloads are not at the edge of memory bandwidth limits, A100 provides good value. H100 is preferred when: maximum throughput is required, FP8 or BF16 peak performance matters, HBM3 bandwidth is needed to avoid ITL saturation, or large batch sizes are required. The choice depends on cost-performance requirements for the specific workload.

questions.question: What is the role of CUDA unified memory in GPU inference?
questions.reference: CUDA unified memory allows the CPU and GPU to share a single address space, with the CUDA runtime managing data movement. For LLM inference it is generally not used for model weights or KV cache — these are explicitly managed in VRAM for performance. Unified memory is used for smaller allocations and framework-level operations. For serving performance, explicit VRAM management (as vLLM does) is far faster than unified memory for hot data like weights and KV cache.

questions.question: What does --enforce-eager do in vLLM and when would you use it?
questions.reference: --enforce-eager disables torch.compile and runs in PyTorch eager (interpreted) mode. This means faster startup (no kernel compilation warmup) at the cost of lower peak throughput and higher ITL. Use it when: fast startup is required; you are running a quick experiment or smoke test; or the model architecture is not well-supported by torch.compile. For production serving where startup time is less critical, compiled mode (without --enforce-eager) delivers significantly better performance.

questions.question: What is the --served-model-name flag and why is it useful?
questions.reference: --served-model-name sets the model name that vLLM reports in its OpenAI-compatible API. When clients send requests, they specify a model name in the payload. This flag allows you to serve a model under an alias (e.g., 'gpt-4') while actually running a different model, enabling drop-in compatibility with clients that expect specific model names. It does not change the model loaded — only the name reported by the API.

questions.question: What does --tensor-parallel-size control?
questions.reference: --tensor-parallel-size sets the number of GPUs across which the model's weight tensors are sharded. With tp=1, the model runs on a single GPU. With tp=4, the model is split across 4 GPUs and all 4 cooperate on every forward pass via NVLink all-reduce communication. Tensor parallelism is required when a model is too large to fit on a single GPU. For models that fit on one GPU, replicas are more efficient than tensor parallelism.

questions.question: What does --limit-mm-per-prompt do in vLLM?
questions.reference: --limit-mm-per-prompt restricts how many multimodal inputs (images, videos) a single request can contain. For example, {"image": 1, "video": 0} limits each request to one image and zero videos. This prevents a single request from consuming excessive VRAM for vision encoder processing. For text-only requests this flag is ignored. It is important for multimodal models to prevent resource exhaustion from requests with many images.

questions.question: What is the --uvicorn-log-level flag and what should it be set to in production?
questions.reference: --uvicorn-log-level controls the verbosity of the Uvicorn HTTP server logs. Options include debug, info, warning, error, critical. In production, set it to warning or error to reduce log volume and I/O overhead. In development or debugging, info or debug provides more visibility. Excessive logging at high request rates can itself become a performance bottleneck, so production deployments should minimise log verbosity.

questions.question: What does --dtype control and what are the valid options?
questions.reference: --dtype sets the data type for model weights and activations. Options: auto (uses model's default), float16, bfloat16, float32, fp8. bfloat16 is recommended for H100 GPUs — it is numerically stable (wide dynamic range) and runs on BF16 tensor cores. float16 is an alternative but can be less stable for some models. float32 doubles memory usage and is rarely used for inference. fp8 is supported on H100 and offers higher throughput for compute-bound workloads.

questions.question: What does --max-model-len do and what happens if a request exceeds it?
questions.reference: --max-model-len sets the maximum number of tokens (prompt + output) a single request can use. If a request's prompt alone exceeds max_model_len, vLLM returns an error and rejects the request. This limits KV cache size per sequence. Setting it too low rejects valid long requests; too high wastes VRAM on KV cache allocation for lengths that rarely occur. It should be set based on the 99th percentile of expected sequence lengths in the workload.

questions.question: What is the --host and --port flag used for in vLLM?
questions.reference: --host sets the network interface vLLM binds to. Use 0.0.0.0 to accept connections from any network interface (required for external access). 127.0.0.1 restricts to localhost only. --port sets the TCP port (default 8000). In Modal's web server setup, vLLM must bind to 0.0.0.0 and the Modal web_server decorator must specify the same port so Modal's proxy can forward external traffic to the vLLM process.

questions.question: What is the --gpu-memory-utilization flag and what is a safe default?
questions.reference: --gpu-memory-utilization (default 0.9) tells vLLM what fraction of total GPU VRAM to allocate for weights + KV cache combined. After loading model weights, the remaining fraction is used for KV cache blocks. 0.9 is a safe default for most scenarios, leaving 10% for CUDA context, activation buffers, and other overhead. Lower to 0.85 if experiencing OOM errors during large prefill batches. Raising above 0.95 risks OOM.

questions.question: What flags would you set to optimise vLLM for a text-only workload versus a multimodal workload?
questions.reference: Text-only: use --enforce-eager for fast startup or omit it for compiled mode; set --limit-mm-per-prompt to restrict images; increase max_num_seqs since text KV caches are smaller. Multimodal: ensure --limit-mm-per-prompt is set appropriately; reduce max_num_seqs since image tokens inflate KV cache; set --mm-processor-kwargs to control image resolution (min_pixels, max_pixels); expect higher VRAM consumption per request due to vision encoder outputs being added to the KV cache.

questions.question: What does the --min-containers and --max-containers parameter control in Modal?
questions.reference: In Modal, min_containers sets the minimum number of always-warm container instances. Setting min_containers=1 keeps at least one GPU container running at all times, eliminating cold start latency for the first request after idle periods. max_containers sets the upper bound on autoscaling. Setting both to the same value (e.g., both=2) creates a fixed replica count. Higher min_containers increases cost but reduces latency variability from cold starts.

questions.question: What is the scaledown_window parameter in Modal and why does it matter for cost?
questions.reference: scaledown_window sets how long (in seconds) a Modal container remains alive after its last request before being terminated. A longer window reduces cold starts (containers stay warm) but increases cost (idle GPU time billed). A shorter window saves money but increases cold start frequency. For production serving, balance this based on request frequency — if requests arrive at least every few minutes, a 10-15 minute scaledown window keeps the container warm without excessive idle cost.

questions.question: What does --no-enforce-eager do differently from the default?
questions.reference: --no-enforce-eager is the flag to explicitly enable compiled mode (torch.compile) when you want to be explicit about it. By default in vLLM, eager mode is used (equivalent to --enforce-eager). Passing --no-enforce-eager enables torch.compile, which compiles GPU kernels for the decode loop. This produces faster inference at the cost of a long initial compilation warmup. In the InferTutor capstone, --no-fast-boot maps to --no-enforce-eager internally.

questions.question: How would you configure vLLM to handle both short and long context requests efficiently?
questions.reference: Enable chunked prefill (--enable-chunked-prefill) to prevent long prefills from blocking short requests. Set max_num_batched_tokens high enough to handle long contexts in reasonable time. Set max_model_len to the actual maximum needed (not excessively high). Enable prefix caching (--enable-prefix-caching) if requests share common prefixes like system prompts. Monitor TTFT distribution — if p99 is much higher than p95, long requests are causing tail latency for concurrent short requests.

questions.question: What happens if concurrent_inputs in Modal is set lower than max_num_seqs in vLLM?
questions.reference: If concurrent_inputs < max_num_seqs, Modal's gateway will reject requests before they reach vLLM, even when vLLM has available decode capacity. This creates an artificial bottleneck at the gateway layer — errors appear not because vLLM is overloaded but because Modal's queue is full. The fix is to always set concurrent_inputs >= max_num_seqs + a buffer (e.g., max_num_seqs * 2) to ensure Modal never rejects requests that vLLM could serve.

questions.question: What is the fundamental difference between replicas and tensor parallelism?
questions.reference: Replicas run independent full copies of the model, each on its own GPU (or set of GPUs). Requests are load-balanced across replicas independently. Tensor parallelism (TP) splits a single model across multiple GPUs that cooperate on every forward pass using all-reduce communication. Replicas scale throughput by parallelising requests; TP scales by parallelising computation within a single request. Replicas have no inter-GPU communication; TP has all-reduce overhead on every layer.

questions.question: When would you choose tensor parallelism over replicas?
questions.reference: Choose tensor parallelism when the model is too large to fit on a single GPU. For example, a 70B BF16 model requires ~140GB VRAM — it cannot fit on a single 80GB H100 and requires TP=2 minimum. For models that fit on one GPU (4B, 7B, 13B), replicas are always preferred because they have zero communication overhead. TP adds all-reduce latency to every forward pass, which increases ITL for every request regardless of concurrency.

questions.question: How does the scoring formula penalise using more GPUs?
questions.reference: The score formula divides by total_gpus: score = goodput * users / (ttft_p95 * itl_p95 * total_gpus). Doubling the GPU count requires more than doubling the score's numerator (goodput * users) just to break even. If 2 GPUs give exactly 2x the throughput and 2x the users but TTFT and ITL stay the same, the score is identical to 1 GPU. Only throughput/user gains that exceed the GPU count multiplier improve the score — making GPU efficiency a core metric.

questions.question: What communication overhead does tensor parallelism introduce?
questions.reference: Tensor parallelism requires all-reduce collective operations between GPUs after each attention layer and after each MLP layer. Each all-reduce synchronises partial computation results across all TP ranks. On H100 GPUs connected by NVLink, this communication is fast (~microseconds), but it still adds fixed latency to every forward pass. With TP=2 the overhead is small; with TP=8 the communication cost becomes significant. Replicas have zero such overhead.

questions.question: For a 4B parameter model on H100 GPUs, what is the recommended scaling strategy?
questions.reference: Use replicas (not tensor parallelism). A 4B BF16 model requires only ~8GB VRAM, leaving ~72GB for KV cache and overhead on an 80GB H100. The model fits comfortably on one GPU. Adding more GPUs via replicas adds more request-handling capacity with zero communication overhead. Tensor parallelism would split the small model across GPUs unnecessarily, adding all-reduce overhead to every request without meaningful benefit.

questions.question: How does load balancing work across replicas in Modal?
questions.reference: Modal's gateway load balances incoming requests across available container replicas. The load balancing is transparent to the client — all replicas share the same public URL. Modal routes each request to a replica based on availability, not content. This means replicas do not share state, KV cache, or prefix cache with each other. Each replica maintains its own independent prefix cache, so cache hit rates are lower than if a single replica served all traffic.

questions.question: What is pipeline parallelism and how does it differ from tensor parallelism?
questions.reference: Pipeline parallelism assigns different transformer layers to different GPUs. GPU 0 processes layers 1-12, GPU 1 processes layers 13-24, etc. Each GPU processes a micro-batch and passes activations to the next GPU. Tensor parallelism splits each individual layer across GPUs. Pipeline parallelism has less communication (only activations between stages) but introduces pipeline bubbles (idle time when waiting for previous stages). TP is more commonly used for LLM inference; pipeline parallelism is more common in training.

questions.question: Why might 4 replicas produce worse results than 1 replica in the scoring formula?
questions.reference: If 4 replicas produce 4x throughput but also have 4x the GPU count, they only break even on score. If errors occur (e.g., due to queue misconfigurations) the score drops further. In practice, 4 replicas at 400 users may produce high error rates if max_num_seqs or concurrent_inputs are not scaled proportionally, causing most requests to be rejected. A high-error 4-GPU run can easily score lower than a zero-error 1-GPU run even with higher raw throughput.

questions.question: What is the GPU_COUNT variable in the vLLM Modal app and how does it relate to tensor parallelism?
questions.reference: GPU_COUNT sets how many GPUs each container instance uses. When GPU_COUNT > 1 and TENSOR_PARALLEL > 1, vLLM uses those GPUs for tensor parallelism within a single replica. When GPU_COUNT=1 and REPLICAS=4, Modal spins up 4 independent containers each with 1 GPU. total_gpus = GPU_COUNT * REPLICAS. For a 4B model, always use GPU_COUNT=1 and scale with REPLICAS.

questions.question: How would you serve a 70B model on H100 GPUs?
questions.reference: A 70B BF16 model requires ~140GB VRAM — does not fit on a single 80GB H100. Options: (1) TP=2 with 2 H100s per replica — splits the model across 2 GPUs using tensor parallelism; (2) INT4 quantisation reduces to ~35GB, fitting on a single H100 enabling replicas instead of TP; (3) TP=4 for maximum throughput per replica at higher cost. For production, INT4 or INT8 + replicas often outperforms TP in score due to lower communication overhead.

questions.question: What is chunked prefill and what problem does it solve?
questions.reference: Chunked prefill breaks a long prefill operation into smaller chunks and interleaves them with decode steps. Without chunked prefill, a long prompt monopolises the GPU during its entire prefill, blocking decode steps for all other running sequences — causing ITL spikes for every concurrent request during that time. With chunked prefill, the GPU alternates between small prefill chunks and decode steps, smoothing ITL at the cost of slightly longer total prefill time per long request.

questions.question: How does chunked prefill affect TTFT?
questions.reference: Chunked prefill slightly increases TTFT for long-prompt requests because prefill is processed in multiple steps (each interleaved with decode) rather than one continuous pass. However, it reduces TTFT variance — short requests no longer experience sudden TTFT spikes caused by a long request monopolising prefill. The net effect on p95 TTFT is often positive because it eliminates tail latency events caused by head-of-line blocking.

questions.question: What is prefix caching and what workloads benefit most from it?
questions.reference: Prefix caching stores computed KV cache blocks for prompt prefixes that have been seen before. On subsequent requests with the same prefix, vLLM reuses the cached blocks instead of recomputing them. Workloads that benefit most: (1) all requests share a long system prompt; (2) multiturn conversations where earlier turns are common across users; (3) few-shot examples repeated across requests. The cache hit rate directly determines the TTFT reduction — 95% hit rate means 95% of prefix tokens are served from cache.

questions.question: What is the cache hit rate and how is it measured in vLLM?
questions.reference: Cache hit rate is the fraction of input tokens that are served from the prefix cache rather than recomputed. vLLM reports this as vllm:cpu_prefix_cache_hit_rate and vllm:gpu_prefix_cache_hit_rate in its Prometheus metrics. A 95% hit rate means 95% of prefill tokens were cached. The hit rate depends on how consistently requests share a common prefix — identical system prompts across all requests produce the highest hit rates.

questions.question: Does prefix caching work across different users' requests?
questions.reference: Yes — prefix caching is request-agnostic. If user A and user B both send requests with the same system prompt, the KV cache computed for user A's system prompt is reused for user B's request. The cache is keyed on the token sequence content, not the user identity. This is why shared system prompts in production deployments with many users produce very high cache hit rates — the cache scales its benefit with the number of users sharing the same prefix.

questions.question: What is the relationship between prefix caching and TTFT reduction?
questions.reference: TTFT = queue wait time + prefill computation time. Prefix caching eliminates prefill computation for cached tokens — they are simply looked up and reused. If a system prompt is 500 tokens and the cache hit rate is 100%, those 500 tokens require zero compute during prefill. For a short follow-up question of 20 tokens, TTFT drops from the cost of processing 520 tokens to just 20 tokens — a substantial reduction.

questions.question: Does prefix caching affect the KV cache memory footprint?
questions.reference: Yes — prefix caching stores KV blocks in memory even after a request completes, anticipating reuse. This increases the steady-state KV cache memory usage compared to a system without prefix caching. If VRAM is tight, prefix caching competes with active sequence KV cache for blocks. vLLM manages this by evicting prefix cache blocks when active sequence demand is high, using an LRU eviction policy. Enable prefix caching only when VRAM allows maintaining a useful cache size.

questions.question: How does chunked prefill interact with max_num_batched_tokens?
questions.reference: max_num_batched_tokens sets the maximum tokens processed per forward pass. With chunked prefill enabled, vLLM splits long prefills into chunks no larger than max_num_batched_tokens, interleaved with decode steps. A smaller max_num_batched_tokens means smaller chunks (smoother ITL but slower per-request prefill). A larger value means larger chunks (faster prefill but potentially more ITL disruption per chunk). Tuning max_num_batched_tokens controls the granularity of the prefill-decode interleaving.

questions.question: What is the difference between CPU prefix cache and GPU prefix cache in vLLM?
questions.reference: vLLM can maintain prefix cache blocks both in GPU VRAM (fast access, limited capacity) and CPU RAM (slower access, larger capacity). GPU prefix cache is served instantly when hit. CPU prefix cache requires swapping blocks from CPU to GPU memory, adding latency but providing more total cache capacity. For most production deployments, GPU prefix caching alone provides sufficient benefit. CPU caching helps when the prefix vocabulary is very large and GPU cache capacity is insufficient.

questions.question: In a mixed workload with short text and long image requests, should chunked prefill be enabled?
questions.reference: Yes — chunked prefill is especially valuable in mixed workloads. Image requests have large prefills (image tokens + text tokens) that would block short text requests during their entire prefill without chunking. With chunked prefill, the long image prefill is broken into chunks interleaved with decode, preventing short text requests from experiencing ITL spikes caused by waiting for an image request's prefill to complete. The benefit is proportional to the length variance in the workload.

questions.question: How would you verify that prefix caching is actually working in production?
questions.reference: Monitor the vllm:gpu_prefix_cache_hit_rate metric from vLLM's /metrics endpoint. A high hit rate (>50%) confirms the cache is working. If hit rate is low despite a shared system prompt, possible causes: (1) requests are not actually sharing the exact same prefix tokens; (2) prefix cache blocks are being evicted due to VRAM pressure; (3) prefix caching is not enabled (check --enable-prefix-caching flag). Also check the logs for cache-related messages during startup.

questions.question: What is the tradeoff between enabling and disabling prefix caching?
questions.reference: Enabling prefix caching: reduces TTFT for requests with shared prefixes, reduces GPU compute for repeated prefills, improves throughput under high load with common system prompts. Tradeoff: uses KV cache VRAM to store prefix blocks that may not be reused, reducing VRAM available for active sequences. Disabling prefix caching: all VRAM goes to active sequences, maximising concurrent capacity. Best strategy: enable prefix caching when the workload has high prefix repetition; disable when VRAM is tight and prefix reuse is low.

questions.question: What is flash attention and how does it improve inference efficiency?
questions.reference: Flash Attention is an optimised attention implementation that fuses multiple attention operations into a single GPU kernel, dramatically reducing memory reads/writes compared to the standard unfused implementation. Standard attention materialises the full attention matrix in HBM (O(n²) memory); Flash Attention computes attention in tiles entirely within GPU SRAM (fast on-chip memory), requiring O(n) HBM reads. This reduces memory bandwidth demand and improves both speed and numerical precision. vLLM uses Flash Attention by default when available.

questions.question: How does online softmax relate to Flash Attention?
questions.reference: Online softmax is a numerical technique that computes the softmax of a sequence in a single pass without first materialising the full attention scores. Standard softmax requires two passes: one to find the maximum (for numerical stability) and one to compute the exponentials. Online softmax folds both passes into one using a running maximum update. Flash Attention uses online softmax to enable tiled attention computation — processing one tile at a time and maintaining running statistics, avoiding full materialisation of the O(n²) attention matrix in HBM.

questions.question: What would you tune first if a team reports that their TTFT is acceptable but their system crashes under sustained load?
questions.reference: Crashes under sustained load with acceptable TTFT typically indicate memory exhaustion, not scheduling issues. Investigate: (1) KV cache filling up — monitor gpu_cache_usage_perc and reduce max_num_seqs or max_model_len; (2) GPU OOM from activation spikes during large prefill batches — lower gpu_memory_utilization to 0.85; (3) prefix cache consuming too much VRAM — disable prefix caching temporarily to test; (4) memory leak in the serving framework — monitor VRAM usage over time with nvidia-smi. Reduce load incrementally until the crash threshold is identified.
